tldr: Lee, Ghassami, and Shpitser prove \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\) sound and leave the \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) line without a graphical countermodel certificate. This core defines a context-specific hedge that requires every supplied selector context to sever one of two parity blocks, and it asks whether the resulting full parity CS-SCM makes all supplied context laws independent of the latent coupling while changing \(p(Y(a,S=\emptyset))\). A conditional frontier theorem then turns the missing \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) converse into the exact failure-if-and-only-if-nonidentification characterization promised by the published completeness conjecture.

symbols.name: \(U_v\)
symbols.def: Exogenous input to the structural equation at \(v\).

symbols.name: \(S\)
symbols.def: Selector vertex with state space \(\mathcal X_S=2^{\mathcal L}\).

symbols.name: \(a\)
symbols.def: Treatment value in the target intervention.

symbols.name: \(u\)
symbols.def: A selector state; \(u=\emptyset\) is the target selector intervention.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal U\)
symbols.def: Selector states whose context laws are supplied.

symbols.name: \(H\)
symbols.def: A directed-and-bidirected edge-copy submultigraph with vertex set \(\operatorname{v}(H)\subseteq V\).

symbols.name: \(\rho_u(H)\)
symbols.def: The context restriction retaining edge copies \(e\in H\) satisfying \(\lambda(e)\cap u=\emptyset\).

symbols.name: \(R\)
symbols.def: Common root set of a rooted \(C\)-forest pair.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal H_{\mathrm{ord}}(\bar G,A)\)
symbols.def: Ordinary rooted hedge pairs for \(\bar G\) and \(A\).

symbols.name: \(D^\star\)
symbols.def: Failed district in a terminal \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\) trace.

symbols.name: \(C^\star\)
symbols.def: Fixing closure of \(D^\star\) in that trace.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal M_{\mathrm{CS}}(\bar G)\)
symbols.def: Finite compatible CS-SCMs with label-set latent projection \(\bar G\).

symbols.name: \(\equiv_{\mathcal U}\)
symbols.def: Equality of all supplied context laws.

symbols.name: \(F_{\mathrm{SS\text{-}ID}}\)
symbols.def: Identifying functional output on a successful \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\) trace.

standard.name: positive probability of conditioned selector states

assumptions.condition: \rho_u(H)=\bigl(\operatorname{v}(H),\{e\in H\cap E^{\to}:\lambda(e)\cap u=\emptyset\},\{e\in H\cap E^{\leftrightarrow}:\lambda(e)\cap u=\emptyset\}\bigr)

novel.justification: This is the exact terminal configuration isolated in the systematic-selection algorithm. It arises when the selector remains in the confounded fixing closure after all context information has been exhausted.

definitions.name: Rooted \(C\)-forest family
definitions.construction: \mathcal F_R(\bar G)=\{H\subseteq\bar G:R\subseteq\operatorname{v}(H),\ H^{\leftrightarrow}\text{ is connected},\ \forall v\in\operatorname{v}(H)\setminus R\ \exists!w\in\operatorname{v}(H)\ (v\to w)\in H,\ \forall r\in R\ \neg\exists w\in\operatorname{v}(H)\ (r\to w)\in H\}

definitions.name: Ordinary hedge class
definitions.construction: \mathcal H_{\mathrm{ord}}(\bar G,A)=\{(F_0,F_1,R):F_0,F_1\in\mathcal F_R(\bar G),\ F_0\subsetneq F_1,\ \operatorname{v}(F_0)\cap A=\emptyset,\ \operatorname{v}(F_1)\cap A\ne\emptyset\}

definitions.name: Compatible CS-SCM class
definitions.construction: \mathcal M_{\mathrm{CS}}(\bar G)=\{M=(\{f_v\}_{v\in V},P_U):X_v=f_v(X_{\operatorname{pa}_{\bar G}(v)},U_v,S)\ \forall v\in V,\ P_U\text{ is finite},\text{ and the label-set latent projection of }M\text{ is }\bar G\}

definitions.name: Terminal confounded-selector class
definitions.construction: \mathcal C_{\mathrm{cs2}}=\{\mathcal I=(\bar G,A,Y,\mathcal U):S\in C^\star\setminus D^\star,\ \mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}(\mathcal I)=\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2\}\}

definitions.name: Context-specific hedge
definitions.construction:
\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\mathcal I)=\{\mathfrak H=(F_0,F_L,F_R,F_1,R,T_L,T_R):(F_0,F_1,R)\in\mathcal H_{\mathrm{ord}}( ar G,A),\ F_0=F_1[\operatorname{v}(F_0)],\ T_L
e\emptyset,\ T_R
e\emptyset,\ T_L\cap T_R=\emptyset,\ T_L\cup T_R=\operatorname{v}(F_1)\setminus\operatorname{v}(F_0),\ F_L=F_1[\operatorname{v}(F_0)\cup T_L],\ F_R=F_1[\operatorname{v}(F_0)\cup T_R],\  orall u\in\mathcal U\ [
eg\operatorname{Conn}_u(T_L,R;F_L)\vee
eg\operatorname{Conn}_u(T_R,R;F_R)],\ \operatorname{Conn}_{\emptyset}(T_L,R;F_L),\ \operatorname{Conn}_{\emptyset}(T_R,R;F_R)\}

definitions.name: Full parity-pasting CS-SCM
definitions.construction: M_\kappa=\operatorname{ParityPaste}(\mathfrak H,\kappa):\ U=(U^0,\{U_e:e\in E^{\leftrightarrow}\},B_L,B_R),\ \Pr_\kappa(B_L=i,B_R=j)=\tfrac14[1+\kappa(-1)^{i+j}],\ U^0,\{U_e\}\perp(B_L,B_R),\ X_v=f_v^0(X_{\operatorname{pa}_{\bar G}(v)},U_v^0,S)\text{ for }v\notin T_L\cup T_R,\ X_v=f_v^0(X_{\operatorname{pa}_{\bar G}(v)},U_v^0,S)\oplus B_L\text{ for }v\in T_L,\ X_v=f_v^0(X_{\operatorname{pa}_{\bar G}(v)},U_v^0,S)\oplus B_R\text{ for }v\in T_R;\ f_v^0\text{ is the Shpitser--Pearl binary response function on }F_1\text{ and the fixed compatible response function on }\bar G\setminus F_1;\ U_e\text{ enters both endpoint equations exactly when }e\text{ is retained by }\rho_S(\bar G)\text{; shared vertices and edges use the unique equations on }F_0=F_1[\operatorname{v}(F_0)]

definitions.name: Context-selected identifiability
definitions.construction: p(Y\mid\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset))\text{ is identifiable from }\{P_u:u\in\mathcal U\}\Longleftrightarrow\forall M,M'\in\mathcal M_{\mathrm{CS}}(\bar G),\ [\forall u\in\mathcal U\ p_M(V\setminus\{S\}\mid S=u)=p_{M'}(V\setminus\{S\}\mid S=u)]\Rightarrow p_M(Y\mid\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset))=p_{M'}(Y\mid\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset))

sampling_model.design: A CS-SCM supplies \(P_u=p(V\setminus\{S\}\mid S=u)\) for each \(u\in\mathcal U\).
sampling_model.units: The population object is one finite CS-SCM, indexed by selector state \(S\in2^{\mathcal L}\).
sampling_model.treatment: The target fixes \(A=a\) and \(S=\emptyset\) by intervention.
sampling_model.overlap: Each supplied selector state has positive probability.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: Not applicable: the proposal concerns population-level graphical identification.

statements.statement: For every \(\mathfrak H\in\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\mathcal I)\), every \(u\in\mathcal U\), every \(x\in\prod_{v\in V\setminus\{S\}}\mathcal X_v\), and every \(\kappa,\kappa'\in[-1,1]\), the full construction satisfies \(P_{u,\kappa}(x)=P_{u,\kappa'}(x)\).
statements.justification: This is the substantive context-uniform step: after a context severs one parity block, its complete observable law must lose the coupling moment.
statements.gap: Ordinary hedge arguments hide a coupling from one observed regime, and surrogate-experiment thickets synchronize supplied regimes, but neither proves invariance after every label-induced graph restriction.
statements.consumer: It supplies the observed-law equality needed for a valid systematic-selection nonidentification witness.

statements.statement: For every \(\mathfrak H\in\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\mathcal I)\), there exists \(\epsilon\in(0,1]\) such that \(M_{\epsilon},M_{-\epsilon}\in\mathcal M_{\mathrm{CS}}(\bar G)\), \(M_{\epsilon}\equiv_{\mathcal U}M_{-\epsilon}\), and \(Q_{\epsilon}\ne Q_{-\epsilon}\).
statements.justification: The theorem elevates a two-block graph certificate to two full compatible CS-SCMs that agree on every supplied law and disagree on the target intervention.
statements.gap: The ordinary parity hedge of Shpitser--Pearl lacks selector contexts, while Lee--Correa--Bareinboim do not address label-dependent deletion of edges and vertices.
statements.consumer: It turns a detected CS-hedge into a certified reason that no functional of the supplied context laws can identify the target.

statements.statement: For every \(\mathcal I\in\mathcal C_{\mathrm{cs2}}\), does there exist \(\mathfrak H\in\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\mathcal I)\) and hence a pair \(M_{\epsilon},M_{-\epsilon}\) from the parity-pasting construction satisfying \(M_{\epsilon}\equiv_{\mathcal U}M_{-\epsilon}\) and \(Q_{\epsilon}\ne Q_{-\epsilon}\)?
statements.justification: This is the remaining converse at the sole published terminal line without a nonidentification certificate.
statements.gap: Lee, Ghassami, and Shpitser explicitly conjecture completeness after treating their other terminal cases, but no published graph object converts \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) into a context-law-equivalent countermodel pair.
statements.consumer: An affirmative answer supplies the one missing premise of the SS-ID completeness frontier.

statements.statement: If Open-ended Question 1 has an affirmative answer, then for every finite CS-SCM instance \(\mathcal I\), \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}(\mathcal I)\) succeeds if and only if \(p(Y\mid\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset))\) is identifiable from \(\{P_u:u\in\mathcal U\}\).
statements.justification: This states the comparator-facing frontier obtained by joining published soundness and covered failure certificates to the only unresolved converse.
statements.gap: Theorem 4 and the \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) discussion of Lee, Ghassami, and Shpitser establish soundness and leave full completeness conjectured; this frontier is strictly tighter only if the open converse is resolved.
statements.consumer: It makes SS-ID a complete decision procedure for the paper's finite systematic-selection identification problem.

statements.statement: If \(\mathcal U=\emptyset\) and \(S\) is fixed to \(\emptyset\), then the underlying pair \((F_0,F_1,R)\) of every \(\mathfrak H\in\mathcal H_{\mathrm{CS}}(\mathcal I)\) is an ordinary hedge, and Theorem 1 reduces to the classical hedge implication of nonidentification for \(p(Y\mid\operatorname{do}(A=a))\).
statements.justification: The no-selection boundary recovers the ordinary hedge witness while retaining the new refinement only where contexts exist.
statements.gap: Classical hedge completeness has no quantifier over selector-induced context laws.
statements.consumer: It checks the new object against the standard ID special case.

project_justification.gap: The systematic-selection algorithm is sound, but its \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) terminal trace lacks the converse certificate required for a complete identification decision.
project_justification.niche: A witness must preserve every positive-probability selector-context law while changing the distribution under the explicit intervention \(\operatorname{do}(A=a,S=\emptyset)\).
project_justification.fill: The CS-hedge records contextwise severing, the full parity construction makes the required countermodels well defined, and the frontier theorem locates the exact remaining premise for completeness.

related_work: Lee, Ghassami, and Shpitser define CS-SCMs and \(\mathsf{SS\text{-}ID}\); their Theorem 4 proves soundness, and their subsequent failure analysis leaves \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) in the stated completeness conjecture. Shpitser and Pearl provide the ordinary hedge and binary parity response-function mechanism used as the within-forest component of the construction. Lee, Correa, and Bareinboim synchronize nonidentification witnesses across supplied surrogate experiments; that result informs, but does not extend to, selector-induced label restrictions. Kivva, Mokhtarian, Etesami, and Kiyavash show why positivity is material for general-identifiability completeness; the present frontier is restricted to finite CS-SCM semantics and positive supplied selector states. Bareinboim and Pearl establish transportability completeness with selection diagrams, but not selection into experimental contexts of a common CS-SCM.
honest_scope: The marginal-invariance and sufficiency claims are conditional on the displayed CS-hedge and full parity construction. The standing open question is whether every exact \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) trace has that certificate. The core does not claim positivity-compatible completeness beyond positive supplied selector states, an efficient certificate extractor, or an unconditional binary-witness theorem.

bibliography.citation: J. J. R. Lee, A. Ghassami, and I. Shpitser. A General Identification Algorithm for Data Fusion Problems under Systematic Selection. UAI, 2024. arXiv:2404.06602.
I. Shpitser and J. Pearl. Identification of Conditional Interventional Distributions. UAI, 2006. arXiv:1206.6876.
S. Lee, J. D. Correa, and E. Bareinboim. General Identifiability with Arbitrary Surrogate Experiments. UAI, 2020, PMLR 115:389--398.
Y. Kivva, E. Mokhtarian, J. Etesami, and N. Kiyavash. Revisiting the General Identifiability Problem. UAI, 2022. arXiv:2206.01081.
E. Bareinboim and J. Pearl. Transportability of Causal Effects: Completeness Results. AAAI, 2012.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: Theorem 4 proves SS-ID sound; the failure analysis proves nonidentification for covered lines and leaves full completeness conjectured, including the \(\mathtt{cs\_fail\_2}\) line.
An ordinary hedge witnesses nonidentification for a classical interventional query.
Hedgelets and thickets synchronize nonidentification witnesses across arbitrary surrogate experiments.
Positivity changes the general-identifiability completeness problem.

literature_checklist.author: Lee, Ghassami, and Shpitser
literature_checklist.one_line: Theorem 4 supplies SS-ID soundness, while the failure discussion leaves completeness conjectured at the terminal confounded-selector line.

literature_checklist.author: Shpitser and Pearl
literature_checklist.one_line: Provides the ordinary hedge and binary parity response-function template.

literature_checklist.author: Lee, Correa, and Bareinboim
literature_checklist.one_line: Develops hedgelets and thickets for synchronized supplied experimental distributions.

literature_checklist.author: Kivva et al.
literature_checklist.one_line: Shows that positivity is consequential for general-identifiability completeness.

literature_checklist.author: Bareinboim and Pearl
literature_checklist.one_line: Establishes completeness for transportability with selection diagrams.
